Puerto Rico · 2017–present

When a storm hits, geography is only part of the story.

Hurricane Maria made landfall on September 20, 2017 as a Category 4 storm, the strongest hurricane to hit Puerto Rico in nearly a century. Between the storm and its aftermath, thousands perished. Electricity was out for 11 months in some communities. Many are still rebuilding.

But the storm didn't hit everyone the same way. Communities already carrying the weight of low income, aging infrastructure, language barriers, and limited healthcare access didn't just face more danger - they recovered differently, more slowly, and with far less support. Looking at social vulnerability factors and the complexity of support systems can help bridge the divide between what services are often offered and what affected communities actually need.

This tool maps two dimensions together: the physical geography of hazard, and the social conditions that determine who survives it, who gets help, and who gets left behind. Exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity. Understanding them in conversation with each other sharpens our picture of real community need.

It's built for researchers, aid organizations, and the communities themselves, to validate what residents already know, and make it legible to those who need to act.

Start here

Most disaster plans only show where the water goes. This section shows where it goes and which communities cannot get out of the way. Turn layers on one at a time. The order you add them changes which towns look most at risk and why - and the result might surprise you.

What are the physical hazard layers? ▼

Physical Hazard Layers

Standard risk maps focus on the coast. Add landslide risk and watch what happens - interior mountain towns often outrank coastal cities. Toggle layers one at a time to see what's actually driving each community's score.
0.00.250.500.751.0
0.0 – 0.25  Low exposure
Minimal multi-hazard overlap. Physical risk is present but not compounding. Fewer than 6 of 78 municipalities sit here.
0.25 – 0.50  Moderate
One dominant hazard type (flood or wind) with limited secondary overlap. Coastal corridor, most northern municipalities.
0.50 – 0.75  Elevated
Two or more hazard types compounding. Interior mountain communities: flood risk often invisible until landslide layer is added.
0.75 – 1.0  Critical
Severe simultaneous exposure. Yabucoa (Cat.4 Maria landfall, 155 mph), Toa Baja (built below sea level), Loíza (flood + surge + wind).
Physical score alone does not determine who needs the most help. Switch to Vulnerability to see the full picture.
Where do the biggest surprises show up? ▼

Key Stories

The deeper picture

Physical damage is just the beginning. The communities that struggle for years are not always the ones that got hit hardest. They are the ones with the fewest resources to recover. This section shows who is actually most exposed once you account for the human side - and what you can do about it.

How does vulnerability distribute across communities? ▼

Social Complexity Builder

Physical maps tell you where the water goes. This tells you who can't get out of the way.

Check social dimensions one by one to watch communities like Utuado and Ciales jump dramatically in priority once economic insecurity, elderly population, and housing fragility are counted.

The goal: understand the interplay of vulnerabilities to understand what people actually need - not just match them to what an organization already has.
🎯
Put it to work: Compare a coastal municipio to an interior one after turning on all layers, then click each to see which organization actions the gap recommends.
0.00.250.500.751.0
0.0 – 0.25  Low social complexity
Strong documentation, healthcare proximity, stable employment, low housing fragility. Populations have institutional access when disaster strikes.
0.25 – 0.50  Moderate
Some barriers: informal employment, documentation gaps, or healthcare distance. Recovery is slower but not structurally blocked.
0.50 – 0.75  High complexity
Multiple compounding barriers. Interior municipalities where elderly population, economic insecurity, and road fragility overlap. Aid reaches last.
0.75 – 1.0  Severe
Structural barriers to every major aid system. Ciales, Maricao, Loíza: informal housing (no FEMA eligibility), no dialysis access, single-road isolation. Most at-risk population in PR.
Score reflects composite of 10 social vulnerability dimensions. Adding layers one at a time reveals which factor is driving each community's number.
What does it look like inside a household? ▼

SVI Simulator

The goal isn't just to see vulnerability. It's to understand who is vulnerable and why, so outreach, resources, and programs can be designed for them.

Choose a community to pre-fill, then drag the levers to build specific household profiles.

The right panel shows exactly how social factors compound physical risk into consequences that standard hazard maps never capture - and what can be done about it.
Sliders pre-fill from data · drag to explore any scenario
When things go wrong

No official risk report shows what happens when two or three systems fail at once. This section lets you see it. Trigger infrastructure failures and watch how community risk levels shift as the cascade spreads. The communities hit hardest are rarely the ones anyone predicted.

What happens when infrastructure fails? ▼

Cascade Lab

The SVI Simulator shows what social vulnerability looks like inside a household. This shows what happens when the systems those households depend on start to fail.

Infrastructure failures don't just damage physical things - they activate the same vulnerabilities the Simulator measures. A power outage isn't just an electrical problem. For a household with a dialysis patient it's a countdown. For a community without savings it's the beginning of a financial crisis that outlasts the storm by years.

The cascade is the mechanism. Social vulnerability is what determines how far it goes.

Socioeconomic Patterns Research-based

These patterns are drawn from post-disaster research on Maria and earlier storms. They represent documented island-wide trends, not precise geographic data, and are modeled as modifiers to community vulnerability scores.
Explore by clicking infrastructure on the map ▼

Explore Mode

👉 Click infrastructure nodes on the map to trigger failures. Map colors show how communities' vulnerability shifts as systems break down, revealing the hidden fragilities that organizations should be monitoring and preparing for before the next event. No official risk report shows what happens when two systems fail at once. This does.

Socioeconomic Patterns Research-based

Drawn from post-Maria research. Documented island-wide trends, not real-time geographic data. Toggling these shifts the vulnerability distribution across communities.
What is already there

Knowing where communities are vulnerable only gets you halfway. This section shows where community-built capacity already exists - solar microgrids, mutual aid networks, food programs built since Maria. Organizations that partner with what is there get much further than ones that arrive as if nothing is.

Where is community capacity already built? ▼

Community Resilience Index

Vulnerability scores tell you where the need is. This layer tells you where community capacity already exists. Solar microgrids, mutual aid networks, food sovereignty built since Maria are disaster infrastructure.
🎯
Put it to work: High-resilience communities are potential partners. Low-resilience, high-vulnerability communities are where pre-event investment matters most. Click any community to see both scores together.

What is already here

This is not an exhaustive list. Puerto Rico's community resilience ecosystem is far larger than what is mapped here: mutual aid networks, community health workers, faith organizations, agricultural cooperatives, and cultural institutions in nearly every municipality. The pins and stories represent documented examples, not a complete inventory.
Sources: Casa Pueblo, Mutual Aid Disaster Relief, DOE Programa Acceso Solar (2024), María Fund, WPI Puerto Rico Project. Data as of late 2024.
0.00.250.500.751.0
0.0 – 0.25  Fragile
Minimal documented community infrastructure. Formal mutual aid absent or inactive. High dependence on federal FEMA pipeline which has documented failure patterns post-Maria.
0.25 – 0.50  Emerging
Some community-led programs, like a food pantry or informal power sharing, but inconsistent and undocumented. Resilience exists but isn't yet infrastructure.
0.50 – 0.75  Active
Documented mutual aid networks, at least one solar project, organized food security. Coordination happened after Maria. Institutions remember the last storm.
0.75 – 1.0  Exemplary
Multi-system community infrastructure: solar microgrid, food sovereignty, mutual aid network, radio or comm independence. Adjuntas (Casa Pueblo), Ciales (CAM).
High resilience does not cancel high vulnerability. It changes what kind of support is needed. Partner with high-resilience communities; invest pre-event in low-resilience ones.
Human Stories

Thirteen households. Eleven municipalities. Same storm, different worlds: the social conditions that determine who survives, who gets help, and who gets left behind.

ⓘ  Composite portraits
These are not portraits of real individuals. Every detail is drawn from documented fieldwork, research, and community accounts. The people are composite; the experiences are real.

Data & Methodology

How vulnerability scores are computed, where data comes from, and how to replicate this tool.

🔬

Field Assessment Framework

Hyperlocal questions that reveal what hazard models miss - drawn from ethnographic research disaster response work, and climate adaptation projects around the world. Click any question to highlight it for use in the field.

♪ Culture as Infrastructure Puerto Rico's hurricanes in song - before, during, and after ↑
♪ Culture as Infrastructure: Puerto Rico's hurricanes in song
A community's music is a map of what it has been through. Songs that reference life in a hurricane context, that document the experience of surviving one, that hold the grief and anger of the aftermath: these are not decoration. They are how a society processes, normalizes, and eventually integrates a hazard into who it is.

Hazards embedded in songs should, ideally, be embedded in how people live and prepare. That's the long arc. Puerto Rico has been working on it for a long time. These songs are evidence of that work.
In the wake of Hurricane Katrina, musicians reached for Louisiana 1927, a Randy Newman song written in 1974 about the Great Mississippi Flood. It had never been their disaster. Then it was, exactly. The song existed before its worst moment. That is precisely what made it available when needed. Puerto Rico has spent decades building its own version of that vocabulary. This playlist is part of it.
Playlist → songs documented on the right
If the player doesn't load, open the playlist directly on Spotify ↗
The long view: hurricanes as part of being Boricua
Lamento BorincanoRafael Hernández · 1929
"Sale loco de contento, con su cargamento, para la ciudad…"
A jíbaro sets out to sell his goods and finds the market silent. Written about economic devastation under US colonial rule, it became one of the first songs people reached for after Maria, a lament so precisely tuned to loss that it needed no updating. The hazard had changed; the feeling had not.
Flow CriolloTraditional / Various
A frame, not a single song. The style and sensibility of Puerto Rican popular music that has always held weather, poverty, displacement, and joy in the same breath. The long tradition of plena and bomba treated hurricanes as facts of life worth naming. That normalization is the precondition for everything else.
What it felt like: during and after
HugoVarious · Hurricane Hugo reference · 1989
Hugo (1989, Category 4) arrived before the generation that faced Maria. Songs referencing Hugo became intergenerational transmission, carrying knowledge forward carried knowledge forward, Younger residents who knew the songs had a felt reference point when Maria hit.
HuracánVarious artists across decades
The word itself has been a lyrical constant across genres: salsa, bolero, reggaeton. Every generation of Puerto Rican musicians has written a hurricane song. This accumulation is the vocabulary: by the time Maria arrived, the island had dozens of cultural reference points for what a storm does to a place and to people.
Rayo de SolPost-Maria · 2017
Written and recorded in the aftermath, while much of the island was still dark. Songs like this one were acts of documentation as much as art, testimony that something had happened here, was still happening, and that someone was paying attention to it from inside the experience rather than outside it.
Una VelitaPost-Maria · 2017
The image of a single candle, the only light in a home during 200 days without power, became a central metaphor of the post-Maria period. Songs built around it captured what large-scale statistics could not: the quality of waiting, of uncertainty, of making a life inside that absence.
Estamos BienBad Bunny · 2017
"Estamos bien, aunque no tengamos luz…"
Released weeks after Maria, while Puerto Rico was still largely without power. "We are fine, even without lights." The phrase is simultaneously defiant and absurd, which is exactly why it worked. It wasn't denial. It was a refusal to be defined by the devastation, broadcast from inside it. Became the first collectively shared post-storm anthem.
El ApagónBad Bunny · 2022 · Un Verano Sin Ti
"Aquí se queda… esto no lo tumba ni un huracán."
Five years after Maria, the lights were still going out. El Apagón (The Blackout) names the power crisis, the exodus, and the political failure behind both. "This stays here, not even a hurricane can knock it down" reframes the storm not as a natural disaster but as a political one with identifiable authors. The video, filmed across Puerto Rico, became a meditation on who leaves and who stays and why.
The reckoning: political anger as social processing
Afilando los CuchillosiLe, Residente, PJ Sin Suela · 2019
"¿Cuándo nos vamos a cansar?"
"Sharpening the knives." Recorded during the Verano del 19 protests that forced Governor Rosselló's resignation, after leaked chats revealed officials mocking hurricane victims while the death toll was being suppressed. The song was written overnight, released the same day, and performed live in front of hundreds of thousands. Grief that had been private became public rage that became action, with a song as the transmission mechanism.
OdioPost-Maria political wave · Various
The naming of anger, odio, hate, in response to the federal and local government's handling of Maria is a specific cultural act. It refuses the language of gratitude that disaster response often imposes on recipients. Music that names anger accurately is part of how a community moves from grief to demand. The songs in this register document that the political consequences of storms are inseparable from the physical ones.
Anthems that found their moment: songs that became something else
Las Caras LindasIsmael Rivera · Tite Curet Alonso
"Las caras lindas de mi gente negra…"
Written to celebrate Afro-Puerto Rican beauty and identity, it became a post-Maria anthem of solidarity and survival, particularly in communities like Loíza, where both Afro-Boricua culture and social vulnerability are highest. This is the Louisiana 1927 pattern exactly: a song written for one purpose that becomes, in a moment of crisis, the only song that says what needs to be said. That re-purposing is cultural infrastructure in action.
PreciosaRafael Hernández · 1937 · Marc Anthony version 1998
"Preciosa te llaman los bardos que cantan tu historia hermosa…"
A declaration of love for Puerto Rico that names what the island has endured, and insists on its beauty anyway. After Maria, Marc Anthony's version circulated constantly. The song functions as a counter-narrative: not denial of devastation, but refusal to let it be the only story.
